intro: Migrating a system from JavaScript to Rust replaces a managed, single-threaded web runtime with a compiled, system-level language prioritizing raw speed and memory safety. This architecture transition is often chosen to build high-performance WebAssembly tools, eliminate non-deterministic garbage collection pauses, or construct bulletproof backend infrastructure. The rewrite requires adopting strict static typing rules and explicit memory ownership models.

diffs.from: Dynamic types evaluated via loose coercion rules at runtime with open prototypical layouts.
diffs.to: Static, strongly typed compilation backed by explicit generic constraints, algebraic data types, and compile-time verification.

diffs.from: Flexible scripting conventions featuring dynamic closures, arrow notation, and loosely ordered executions.
diffs.to: Explicit expression-oriented syntax utilizing explicit type annotations, pattern matching structures, and strict macro systems.

diffs.from: Automated engine heap analysis and background tracking via an internal runtime garbage collector.
diffs.to: Compile-time memory safety governed by an explicit ownership model with strict lifetime and borrowing verification rules.

diffs.from: Built-in event loop scheduling macro/microtask queues using modern native Promises.
diffs.to: Zero-cost asynchronous futures polled via pluggable runtime frameworks like Tokio, compiled into state machines.

diffs.from: Core library managing runtime variables, web data manipulation, and text processing frameworks.
diffs.to: Low-level primitives for memory layouts, thread management, OS interfaces, and optimized container structures.

examples.title: Error Processing Optimization
examples.description: Transforming risky try/catch blocks into compile-time verified Result enum matching structures.

examples.title: Immutable State Control
examples.description: Converting dynamic object collections into explicit, type-safe vector mappings with pattern matching.

tips.title: Understand Memory Ownership
tips.body: Learn Rust's ownership model early. Passing parameters transfers data ownership unless you explicitly pass data references (&).

tips.title: Use Cargo Tooling
tips.body: Replace your npm execution steps with Cargo commands to compile packages and automate tests seamlessly.

tips.title: Map Exceptions to Enums
tips.body: Eliminate open runtime panics; refactor code execution flows around explicit Option and Result enum pipelines.

tips.title: Enforce Strict Immutability
tips.body: Variables are immutable by default in Rust. You must explicitly declare mutable state scopes using the 'mut' keyword.

faqs.question: Can I reuse my existing JavaScript structure?
faqs.answer: No, you must re-architect your data models around structs, traits, and explicit data lifespans to satisfy the compiler requirements.

faqs.question: What is the equivalent of 'null' in Rust?
faqs.answer: Rust has no raw null values. Missing states must be explicitly wrapped inside the Option enum (Some/None) pattern.

faqs.question: How fast is Rust compared to JavaScript?
faqs.answer: Rust compiles down to native machine binaries, delivering orders of magnitude faster execution speeds and significantly lower memory overhead than JS runtime engines.
